segments.text: So in the next class, I'm going to talk about parameter efficient fine tuning methods, which is kind of a more general.
It's a more general version of prompt tuning or prefix tuning. There are methods that tune a small number of parameters to get the model to do something.
And there's a bunch of different parameter efficient tuning methods. Many people may have heard of something like Laura or adapters.
I just talked about prefix tuning. So essentially prompt tuning and prefix tuning are part of this more general class and parameter efficient fine tuning methods.
And so what we can say is actually prompting is fine tuning prompting is a way of fine tuning the model or getting the model to perform a particular task.
And we have this taxonomy of we have prompts and natural language that are created by humans. Actually, maybe I should say manual prompt engineering here.

This was first done in the GPT two paper where they demonstrate that models models consult tasks by doing it this way prompt paraphrasing is a step up from this because it's no longer relying on human engineering and you can, you know, expand to a broader set of prompts.

It can always start with human created prompts. So it's kind of like broader than that discrete prompt search doesn't necessarily need to rely on a paraphrasing model. It could rely on like gradient based models or something else like that to give you something that's not actually natural language.
Kind of just random tokens continuous prompts or prompt tuning is a step up of that multi layer continuous prompts or prefix tuning is a layer above that parameter efficient.
It's more general than that and then you have all training methods so including fine tuning your model.
And so what are the implications of this.
I think so a lot of people when prompting came out they were like prompting methods are very hacky. I don't like how we have to do manual prompt engineering.
It seems like a dark art is opposed to like, you know, actually, you know, some sort of well understood fine tuning method that we could use.

But I actually like them, I like prompting a lot because if anybody is familiar with like Bayesian Bayesian statistics or machine learning we have that concept of like a prior probability over parameters.
And then a probability that we get after after fine tuning the model or after training the model.

And prompts in a way are our first like good prior over neural network models they give us the ability to specify what task the model should be doing or like a general idea of what task the model should be doing before we ask the model to actually do the task.
And so we can either use that prior as is we can use a prompted model as is without doing any additional tuning.
Or we could take the prior that we have given to the model by using a natural language description of the task it should be doing.
And then combine it with fine tuning so we can take the prompted model.
And initialize we can initialize the distribution of this like test a prompt using the prompt using a human created prompt and then go ahead and fine tune it on lots of training data as well.

And there's a method for doing that by chicken shirts called pattern exploiting training where they do exactly that they basically initialize with a manually created prompt and then they find to get the model on the bottom line.
And then they give us a prompt and based method so they give us this like really nice way to very quickly create a system but we can also have you know whatever level of additional training on that.
So that's a little bit early.
